_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-22R Diphenhydramine (Revised 12/10/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2026). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-22R: Diphenhydramine — MDHHS Approved 1/5/2026, Reviewed 2026
indications: Anaphylaxis (adjunct to epinephrine — not a replacement)
Extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) — dystonic reaction from antipsychotic or antiemetic medications
contraindications: Nausea/vomiting in patients <2 years old or <12 kg (pediatric safety concern)
precautions: Causes drowsiness — monitor mental status and airway
Anticholinergic effects: urinary retention, dry mouth, tachycardia
Elderly patients: increased risk of confusion and delirium
Pediatric N/V indication: only for patients >2 years AND >12 kg
expected_effects: Reduction in histamine-mediated anaphylaxis symptoms (urticaria, itching, angioedema — secondary to epinephrine)
Antiemetic effect for nausea and vomiting
side_effects: Anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention, blurred vision, tachycardia)
notes: Anaphylaxis: 50 mg IV/IM — ADJUNCT only; epinephrine is the primary treatment; do not delay epi to give diphenhydramine
N/V adult: 12.5-25 mg IV/IM; maximum 25 mg
N/V pediatric: 1 mg/kg IV/IM; maximum 25 mg; only >2 yr AND >12 kg
Extrapyramidal (EPS): 50 mg adult IV/IM; 1 mg/kg pediatric IV/IM max 50 mg

dosing.notes: ADJUNCT — epinephrine is primary; do not delay epinephrine to administer diphenhydramine
Only for patients >2 years AND >12 kg; not for N/V in younger or smaller patients
